planting.note: Direct sow ripe seeds in fall as they require cold stratification to germinate — do not store seeds; harvest and sow fresh; anise root thrives in moist, rich, shaded woodland soil and self-seeds freely once established.

directions: Anise root is a native woodland perennial of moist, rich, shaded forests across the eastern United States and Canada — every part of the plant, from root to leaf to seed, is intensely fragrant with a sweet, anise-licorice scent that is among the most pleasant of any native wildflower. The thick, fleshy, white taproots are edible raw or cooked and taste strongly of anise; the seeds are used as a spice similar to caraway or anise; and the leaves are used fresh as an anise-flavored garnish and in teas. It is one of the finest shade-garden herbs available, thriving in deep, moist woodland conditions under hardwood canopy where most culinary herbs fail, and it attracts a wide variety of native pollinators to its small white flower umbels in late spring. Sow seeds immediately after ripening in fall — seeds lose viability rapidly if dried and stored; once established, it spreads by self-seeding to form a pleasant aromatic colony in the woodland garden.
sources: Edible and Medicinal Plants of the West (Gregory Tilford)

common_name: Starry Solomon's Seal (Star-Flowered False Solomon's Seal)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; starry Solomon's seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form colonies — plant 6-inch-deep rhizome divisions horizontally and allow several years to establish before dividing.

directions: Starry Solomon's seal is a native woodland perennial closely related to the more common false Solomon's seal (Maianthemum racemosum) but producing star-shaped white flowers spaced individually along the arching stems rather than in a terminal plume — the small, starry flowers have a delicate, precise symmetry that rewards close inspection. Like its relatives, it grows in moist to dry, rich woodland soil across a broad range of habitats from Pacific coast to Atlantic, forming slowly expanding colonies of arching, parallel-veined stems that emerge fresh green in spring and age to attractive copper-bronze in fall. Small striped berries (green to red-striped when young, darkening to deep red or black at maturity) are consumed by thrushes, towhees, and small mammals in fall; the berries and foliage are mildly toxic to humans and should not be consumed. Its compact stature and tolerance of dry shade make it one of the most versatile native groundcovers for both moist and moderately dry woodland gardens — use as an edging or naturalizing groundcover under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; southern spiderwort naturalizes freely in shaded gardens by self-seeding and division of the fibrous root clumps.

directions: Southern spiderwort is a shade-tolerant native Tradescantia of the moist, rich, rocky slopes and ravines of the Appalachian foothills and Ozarks to the Gulf Coastal Plain — the species name 'subaspera' means 'somewhat rough,' referring to the slightly hairy stems and leaves that distinguish it from the smooth Ohio spiderwort. Like other spiderworts, it produces vivid three-petaled flowers of brilliant blue-violet with bright yellow stamens that open in the morning and close by midday, continuing to produce new flowers throughout a long season from late spring through summer; each flower lasts only a few hours but the plant produces dozens in succession. It grows well in the partial to moderate shade of mature deciduous trees and is one of the few genuinely shade-tolerant native wildflowers with a long bloom season — most shade perennials bloom only briefly in spring. The clumping habit and bold, strap-like leaves provide attractive foliage interest even when not in flower; divide clumps every 2–3 years in fall to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, very well-drained soil in full sun; cobaea penstemon is a calcicole (limestone lover) that absolutely requires sharp drainage and does not tolerate clay or wet soils — excellent for rock gardens and dry limestone slopes.

directions: Cobaea penstemon bears the largest flowers of any native American penstemon — its tubular, inflated blooms in shades of lavender to pale purple (occasionally white) with purple guidelines are two inches or more across, giving a tropical richness that seems almost too spectacular for a native of dry limestone prairies. Native to the rocky, alkaline prairies and cedar glades of Oklahoma, Texas, Kansas, and Missouri, it is one of the premier spring-blooming wildflowers of the southern Great Plains, with flower spikes rising from handsome rosettes of glossy, evergreen leaves in April and May. Bumblebees force their way into the large floral tubes to reach nectar, and the plant is an outstanding spring pollinator plant in dry, sunny native gardens. Sharp drainage on limestone or alkaline sandy soil is non-negotiable for this species in cultivation — it thrives in raised beds, rock gardens, and dry stone walls where water runs away quickly; it dies from root rot in typical garden soils with any tendency toward moisture retention.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; smooth penstemon adapts to moist woodland edges as well as dry upland sites in the Southeast, making it more versatile than most penstemons for Gulf Coast garden conditions.

directions: Smooth penstemon is the most widely distributed native penstemon in the southeastern United States, found in open woodland edges, roadside banks, and moist to dry uplands from Virginia to Florida and west to Texas — it is one of the more adaptable penstemons for Gulf Coast gardens, tolerating the heat and humidity of the Deep South better than many western species that struggle in the muggy Southeast. The whorled, tubular lavender-to-white flowers appear in April through June and are eagerly worked by large native bumblebees, hummingbirds, and long-tongued native bees; the plant's smooth, glossy leaves (the 'laevigatus' means smooth) and upright flower spikes provide attractive structure in native garden borders. It adapts to both moist woodland edges with partial shade and dry, sunny upland slopes — a versatility unusual among penstemons that makes it a reliable choice for varied garden conditions in the Southeast. Self-seeds modestly and forms small colonies over time; divide crowded clumps in fall every 3–4 years.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry to average, well-drained, acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy penstemon is exceptionally drought tolerant and performs well in rocky, poor, infertile soils where other plants struggle.

directions: Hairy penstemon is a widely adaptable native penstemon of rocky outcrops, dry meadows, open woodlands, and roadsides in the northeastern and north-central United States, producing spikes of pale violet to whitish, hairy-coated tubular flowers in late spring that are a critical food source for bumblebee queens emerging from winter dormancy and seeking early-season pollen and nectar. The plant is covered in fine hairs from the stems to the flower tubes — hence 'hirsutus' — giving it a soft, gray-green, velvety texture that is attractive in its own right; rosettes of glossy, toothed leaves persist year-round as a semi-evergreen groundcover in milder zones. It is one of the most drought tolerant and cold hardy of all eastern penstemons, adapting to virtually any dry, well-drained site in zones 3–8 with no supplemental irrigation once established; excellent for rock gardens, dry slopes, and hell-strip plantings in regions with cold, dry winters. Self-seeds moderately to form naturalized colonies in suitable rocky or sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas false dragonhead spreads by rhizomes and self-seeds to form colonies in appropriate conditions — provide ample moisture for best performance.

directions: Texas false dragonhead is a rare native perennial of Texas endemic to rocky, moist, calcareous prairies and streamside meadows in the Edwards Plateau and surrounding regions — producing flower spikes similar to the more widely known obedient plant (Physostegia virginiana) but with deeper, brighter pink-purple flowers and a more compact, heat-adapted growth habit suited to south-central Texas conditions. Like other Physostegia species, the individual tubular flowers are hinged and will stay in position when pushed — the 'obedient' character that gives the related species its common name — making them a quirky, interactive feature of native garden tours. Pollinators, especially bumblebees and hummingbirds, visit the tubular, orchid-like flowers throughout summer; in gardens it provides a reliable mid-season pollinator spike when many spring bloomers have finished. It spreads by rhizomes similarly to its relatives and can form assertive colonies in moist, rich soil — site where spreading is welcome or divide regularly to control.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; plains bee balm is an annual or short-lived perennial that self-seeds reliably in dry, open conditions — sow in masses on poor, sandy soil where it can naturalize without competition from taller plants.

directions: Plains bee balm is the drought-tolerant Monarda of the southern Great Plains, growing naturally on dry, sandy, rocky soils of the short-grass and mixed-grass prairies of Texas, Oklahoma, Kansas, and Colorado where rainfall is too scarce and the climate too extreme for other bee balms to survive. The plant is intensely aromatic — a strong, pleasant oregano-like scent rises from the leaves when brushed — and it has been used by indigenous peoples and settlers as a culinary herb and tea plant; it makes an excellent substitute for Mexican oregano in southwestern cooking. Small, pink to lavender whorled flowers appear on branching stems in summer and are heavily visited by native bees and small butterflies for nectar; the aromatic oils deter deer and most mammalian herbivores. In dry, sunny native garden situations and xeriscape designs, plains bee balm provides both ornamental interest and aromatic culinary value with essentially no irrigation required once established in sandy, well-drained soil.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; white bee balm is more shade-tolerant than other Monarda species and thrives in the dappled shade of a woodland edge or shrub border.

directions: White bee balm is the most shade-tolerant of all native bee balms — the only species that thrives in partial shade under deciduous trees rather than requiring full sun — and produces whorls of pure white to pale lavender flowers on upright stems from June through August, providing a critical nectar source for native bumble bees, hummingbird moths, and butterflies in shaded garden areas where most pollinator plants fail. Its strong basil-like fragrance (the 'clinopodia' means 'resembling basil') makes it useful as a culinary herb and potpourri ingredient; leaves dried at peak bloom make an aromatic tea with a floral, oregano-like character. Unlike red bee balm (Monarda didyma) and wild bergamot (M. fistulosa), white bee balm is less aggressive and more restrained in its spread, forming tidy clumps rather than rampaging colonies; it is also more resistant to the powdery mildew that plagues its relatives in humid conditions. An excellent choice for native shade gardens under mature hardwoods where its white flowers provide elegant, fragrant bloom from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in fall in moist to wet, sandy or loamy, acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; narrowleaf false dragonhead is a wetland-edge species that thrives in moist bottomland meadows, streambanks, and the margins of wet prairies.

directions: Narrowleaf false dragonhead is a slender-leaved Physostegia of moist prairies, bottomland meadows, and streambank edges in the central United States — as its name suggests, its leaves are much more narrow and grass-like than those of the common obedient plant (Physostegia virginiana), giving the plant a more delicate, fine-textured appearance in the garden. It blooms in late spring and summer with typical Physostegia spikes of pink-lavender tubular flowers that attract bumblebees and hummingbirds, and like all members of the genus the individual flowers can be physically moved to different positions on the spike — the 'hinged' or 'obedient' characteristic that makes Physostegia one of the more interactive wildflowers for garden visitors. It spreads by rhizomes and self-seeding in moist conditions, forming loose colonies along stream edges and wet meadow borders where it provides good erosion control and continuous summer bloom. In rain garden borders and wet, sunny meadow plantings, it naturalizes pleasantly without becoming overly aggressive.
